Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd just kind of study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Most of my studying happened in bursts—I'd put in a lot of hours one week, then barely touch anything the next. I know I logged about 31 hours a week on average, but it never felt structured or planned out. I learn best by listening anyway, so I'd often just have lectures playing while doing other stuff.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd cram before deadlines and then coast afterward. Regular schedules bore me to death, so I never stuck to one. Sometimes I'd study at 2 AM, other times in the middle of the afternoon—whatever felt right in the moment. The inconsistency didn't really hurt my grades, so I never saw a reason to change it.
Q3: Usually it was because an assignment was coming up or I needed to check if there was something I missed. Sometimes I'd get an email notification and that would remind me to log in. It wasn't like I was excited to go on there—it was more of a 'better check this before I forget' kind of thing. The platform itself didn't really pull me in; it was just a necessary tool.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a groove where logging in felt automatic or natural. It always felt like a chore I had to remember to do. Some weeks I'd log in a bunch, other weeks barely at all. I guess the gamified tasks they added were supposed to make it more routine, but they just felt kind of pointless to me.
Q5: The recorded lectures were the most useful because I could listen to them while doing other things. I'm an auditory learner, so hearing the professor explain concepts stuck with me better than reading. The discussion posts were okay too—sometimes other students would phrase things in a way that clicked better than the official materials. But honestly, I didn't need much from the LMS to do well.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the supplementary readings and the extra practice quizzes. They felt like busywork, and I already understood the material well enough from the lectures. The gamified stuff they introduced didn't interest me either—earning badges or points felt childish. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, I probably skipped it.
Q7: It had to either be graded or genuinely teach me something I didn't already know. If I could tell within the first few minutes that an activity was just filler, I'd bail. I don't like wasting time on things that don't move the needle. When something was actually challenging or connected to the exam content, I'd put in the effort.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable. Required activities at least had consequences if I didn't do them, so I'd make sure to complete those. But optional? I'd look at them and think 'why bother' if my grade was already fine. There was no real incentive to go above and beyond when I was already performing well.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download the lecture audio and listen to it on my phone while walking around or lying in bed. I also used some YouTube videos and external podcasts that explained concepts better than the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely. The LMS only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be thinking through problems or reviewing concepts in my head without being logged in anywhere. I'd listen to downloaded lectures during my commute or while eating. Sometimes I'd discuss course topics with friends in person. None of that shows up in any system, but it was real studying.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video once, download or screenshot what I needed, and then never go back to it on the LMS. Why would I keep logging in when I already had the material saved? It's just more efficient to work offline. My engagement looked low online, but I had everything I needed stored locally.
Q12: There were stretches where the material just felt incredibly boring, and I couldn't force myself to care. The anxiety about keeping my grades up was always there in the background, but the actual content didn't excite me. When I hit those walls, I'd do the bare minimum to stay on track and nothing more. It wasn't about being overwhelmed—I just couldn't muster any enthusiasm.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor. I'd stay up late, sleep in, and then feel too groggy to engage properly with the LMS. When I was tired, I'd just skim through things without really absorbing them. Competing responsibilities weren't a huge issue since I don't do extracurriculars, but my own lack of energy definitely impacted how much I engaged.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials while my mind was completely elsewhere. Sometimes I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to technically complete them without actually paying attention. Being logged in didn't mean I was learning—it just meant I was going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed things all the time, especially discussion posts and optional readings. Even when I knew something was important, if it didn't have an immediate deadline, I'd push it off. The anxiety about it would build up, but I'd still procrastinate until the last minute. It's a bad habit, but somehow I always pulled through in the end.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually do anything on the LMS. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd usually log in more frequently as a deadline approached, cram everything in, and then disappear again afterward. My entire engagement pattern was shaped by when things were due.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but not as much as you'd think. If I got good feedback, I'd feel validated and maybe slack off a bit. If feedback pointed out issues, I'd address them for the next assignment but not fundamentally change my approach. It was more reactive than proactive—I wasn't seeking out feedback to improve, just responding when it came.
Q18: The structure gave me clear deadlines, which was the main thing keeping me on track. But the weekly modules and suggested schedules didn't really influence me—I'd do things in whatever order made sense to me. The gamified elements they added felt tacked on and didn't change how I participated. I worked around the structure rather than within it.
Q19: Having recorded lectures made things easier since I could listen on my own time. But the LMS interface itself was kind of clunky and boring, which made me less inclined to spend time there. The gamified tasks were supposed to help, but they felt patronizing rather than motivating. A more streamlined, less cluttered design would've helped.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for something—maybe they're confused and clicking around trying to figure things out, but never actually completing anything. Or they could be like me, where they access stuff once, take what they need, and work offline. High activity doesn't necessarily mean productive activity.
Q21: That's literally me. I performed well despite barely showing up on the LMS because I studied in ways that weren't tracked. I downloaded materials, listened offline, and thought through problems on my own. The LMS activity doesn't capture how someone actually learns—it just captures clicks and logins. My grades prove that low engagement metrics don't tell the whole story.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when people log in and what they click on, but they miss everything that happens offline or in someone's head. They can't measure understanding, genuine interest, or the quality of someone's thinking. A student could have perfect log data and still not learn anything, while someone like me barely shows up online but aces the exams.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material—not just going through the motions. It's about whether the content sticks and whether you can apply it. Clicking through an LMS isn't engagement; it's compliance. Real engagement is when you're thinking about the course even when you're not logged in.
Q24: The anxiety I deal with doesn't show up in any log. Neither does the boredom that makes me drift away from the material, or the way I genuinely understand concepts despite minimal visible effort. My offline studying, my mental processing, my actual comprehension—none of that gets captured. The data makes me look disengaged when really I'm just efficient and working in ways the system can't see.
